answer:
For adults with asthma, the preferred reliever therapy to reduce exacerbation risk is low-dose ICS-formoterol, as recommended by the Global Initiative for Asthma Report (GINA) in 2024 [1]. This is in contrast to short-acting beta-agonist alone, even at the mildest step of treatment.

[1] Global Strategy for Asthma Management, GINA, 2024.

arguments.query: reliever therapy for adults with asthma to reduce exacerbation risk

result.query: reliever therapy for adults with asthma to reduce exacerbation risk

results.content: For adults and adolescents with asthma, as-needed low-dose ICS-formoterol is preferred as reliever therapy over short-acting beta-agonist alone, even at the mildest step of treatment, to reduce exacerbation risk.
results.citation: Global Strategy for Asthma Management, GINA, 2024

results.content: Aspirin for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease is not routinely recommended due to bleeding risk, except in select high-risk adults 40-70 years after a risk-benefit discussion with their clinician.
results.source: ACC/AHA Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease Guideline
results.citation: Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease, ACC/AHA, 2019

results.content: High-intensity statin therapy is recommended for patients with clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and for primary prevention in adults 40-75 years with LDL-C >=190 mg/dL or diabetes with additional risk factors.
For outpatient community-acquired pneumonia in healthy adults, amoxicillin or doxycycline is recommended. For inpatients, beta-lactam plus macrolide combination therapy is standard.
For moderate to severe depression, a combination of antidepressant medication and high-intensity psychological therapy such as cognitive behavioral therapy is recommended over either treatment alone.
results.source: NICE Guideline on Depression in Adults
results.citation: Management of Depression in Adults, NICE, 2022

results.content: In patients with type 2 diabetes and chronic kidney disease, an SGLT2 inhibitor is recommended for those with eGFR >=20 mL/min/1.73m2 to reduce CKD progression and cardiovascular risk. ACE inhibitors or ARBs are first-line for patients with albuminuria and hypertension.
results.source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
results.citation: Chronic Kidney Disease in Diabetes, American Diabetes Association, 2024

results.content: An A1C goal for many nonpregnant adults with diabetes of <7% without significant hypoglycemia is appropriate. Metformin is the preferred initial pharmacologic agent for type 2 diabetes.
results.source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
results.citation: Glycemic Targets & Pharmacologic Approaches, American Diabetes Association, 2024

results.content: For COPD patients with frequent exacerbations, LABA+LAMA combination therapy is preferred. Inhaled corticosteroids are added for patients with blood eosinophils >=300 cells/uL or continued exacerbations.
results.citation: COPD Diagnosis, Management and Prevention, GOLD, 2024

results.content: A blood pressure target of <130/80 mmHg is recommended for most adults with diabetes and hypertension. ACE inhibitors or ARBs are preferred first-line agents, particularly in the presence of albuminuria.
results.source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
results.citation: Hypertension Management in Diabetes, American Diabetes Association, 2024

results.content: A blood pressure target of <130/80 mmHg is recommended for most adults with hypertension. First-line agents include thiazide diuretics, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and calcium channel blockers.

result.disclaimer: Evidence excerpts for professional review; not a diagnosis.
